narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 55.8% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is just above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction was based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, which is below the typical 3.21 average; your LSAT score of 38, slightly above the average of 36.7; lower-middle family income compared to the dataset norm; and your rankings in school deciles—3rd in Year 1 (versus an average of 5.71) and 1st in Year 3 (far below the 5.53 average). As a Black female planning to study full-time, your background aligns with some underrepresented groups in the data, where White and male students make up the majority. The model weighed these factors carefully, with your Year 3 school decile ranking as the most influential, strongly pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure due to its very low standing relative to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which also nudged the outlook toward failure despite being solid, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, family income bracket, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender. Each of these elements contributed in the direction of increased failure risk, with no single factor overwhelmingly dominating but together tipping the balance. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your academic deciles and GPA stand out as notably lower, while your LSAT offers a relative strength that wasn't enough to offset the others. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many students overcome similar profiles through targeted effort. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on realistically improvable areas like retaking the LSAT for an even higher score or building stronger academic habits that could shine in law school grades, potentially lifting those decile rankings in subsequent years. Your family income and background are harder to change, but seeking scholarships or support networks could help indirectly. Consider part-time options if full-time added any perceived risk, though it's common. With persistence, revisiting these strengths and addressing the academic gaps could flip the prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown capability with that LSAT.
